title.en: Śūnya — zero and decimal place value

dating.display: zero-as-number codified by 628 CE (Brahmagupta); earliest firmly dated written zero-in-place-value in India: 876 CE (Gwalior); Bakhshālī manuscript folios C14-dated 224–993 CE (contested)
dating.basis: Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE) gives explicit arithmetic rules for zero as a number. The Chaturbhuj temple inscription at Gwalior (876 CE) is epigraphically firm. The Bakhshālī manuscript's C14 dates are genuinely contested — both positions listed.

scientific.result: Bodleian Libraries, 2017: folios dated to 224–383 CE, 680–779 CE, and 885–993 CE — potentially pushing the written dot-zero centuries earlier.

scientific.method: Codicological / palaeographic critique of the C14 split
scientific.result: Specialists argue the manuscript is a codicological unity (single scribe/period, likely 8th–10th c. CE) and that the three divergent C14 dates cannot each date the text written on them.

source.label: Plofker, Keller, Hayashi, Montelle & Sarma, "The Bakhshālī Manuscript: A Response to the Bodleian Library's Radiocarbon Dating", History of Science in South Asia 5 (2017)

summary: Indian mathematics turned 'nothing' into a number: a placeholder dot/circle inside a decimal positional system, then a full arithmetic object with rules for adding, subtracting and multiplying by it (Brahmagupta, 628 CE — including the honest failure point: he could not settle 0÷0). The Gwalior inscription of 876 CE shows '270' with a zero, carved in stone, firmly dated.
whyItMatters: Zero plus decimal place value is arguably the single most consequential export in the history of mathematics: every digital device and every ledger on Earth runs on it. It is also the atlas's model case for honest dating — a firm core (628/876 CE) with a genuinely contested earlier horizon (Bakhshālī), both shown.
honestyNote: Other cultures had placeholder zeros (Babylonian sexagesimal placeholder; the Maya, independently). The Indian contribution is specifically zero as a NUMBER inside decimal place value, plus the transmission that made it global. Precision here is what keeps the claim unassailable.

locations.name: Chaturbhuj temple inscription, Gwalior Fort, Madhya Pradesh, India
Bakhshālī manuscript — found near Mardan (KPK, Pakistan); held at the Bodleian Library, Oxford
locations.note: current holder: Bodleian Library, MS. Sansk. d. 14

throughLines.to: The global number system (and hence all digital computation)
chain: Brahmagupta, Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE): arithmetic rules for zero and negatives
Severus Sebokht, Syriac bishop (662 CE): praises 'the nine signs' of Indian reckoning — Indian numerals already noted west of India
al-Khwārizmī, 'On Calculation with Hindu Numerals' (c. 825 CE), Baghdad — Arabic sources call the system ḥisāb al-hind, 'Indian reckoning'
Latin translations (12th c.), 'algorismus'; Fibonacci's Liber Abaci (1202) popularizes it for European commerce
The 'Hindu-Arabic' numerals become the world's numerals

sources.label: Colebrooke, H.T. (tr.), Algebra... from the Sanscrit of Brahmegupta and Bháscara (1817)
Burnett, C., "The Semantics of Indian Numerals in Arabic, Greek and Latin", Journal of Indian Philosophy 34 (2006)
Chrisomalis, S., Numerical Notation: A Comparative History (Cambridge, 2010)
Plofker et al., History of Science in South Asia 5 (2017) — Bakhshālī response
Datta, B. & Singh, A.N., History of Hindu Mathematics (1935)
